name: Dinah Washington
abstract: Dinah Washington (; born Ruth Lee Jones; August 29, 1924 – December 14, 1963) was an American singer and pianist, one of the most popular Black female recording artists of the 1950s. Primarily a jazz vocalist, she performed and recorded in a wide variety of styles including blues, R&B, and traditional pop music, and gave herself the titl…

about_quotes.speaker: David N. Baker
about_quotes.evidence: You probably would have been listening to Count Basie. And a little later, by the 1940s, Ruth Brown, Ray Charles, Dinah Washington, and Nat – of course Nat “King” Cole.
about_quotes.doc_title: David N. Baker — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Junior Mance Part I
about_quotes.evidence: I worked with Dinah Washington for two weeks... I had no problems with Dinah. She gave me complete freedom as to how to play behind her.
about_quotes.doc_title: Junior Mance Part I interviewed by Monk Rowe and Michael Woods, New York City, New York, July 27, 1995 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Lionel Hampton
about_quotes.evidence: So I hired him and for two years I had Dinah Washington and Joe Williams for a boy and girl singer.
about_quotes.doc_title: Lionel Hampton interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, October 18, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Jay Cee Johnson
about_quotes.evidence: Dinah Washington, Ethel Waters, Josh White, Paul Young, Paul Whiteman, Lester Young and others.

about_quotes.speaker: David Albert Francis
about_quotes.evidence: With Dinah Washington, I did "What A Difference a Day Makes" which was a big hit record

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Cobb
about_quotes.evidence: When I was with Dinah Washington... I used to hook up her corset... I was traveling with her and our trio... I met all the bandleaders and all the people that we played with.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Cobb — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
